---
title: "Introduction"
Author: "Mikkel E. Steentoft"
format: html
theme: cosmo
editor: visual
bibliography: references.bib
csl: acta-diabetologica.csl
---

The global prevalence of type 2 diabetes (T2D) and associated complications continues to rise, indicating that existing prevention and treatment strategies are insufficient to halt disease progression [@saeedi2019; @ahlqvist2018]. This may partly reflect that T2D is a more complex and heterogeneous disease than previously assumed, and that a diagnosis based only on glucose levels may not cover underlying pathophysiological drivers. Data-driven phenotyping have begun to characterize this complexity, identifying distinct subgroups with different pathological characteristics and risk-trajectories for diabetes-related complications [@ahlqvist2018; @misra2023; @li2024]. Emerging evidence suggests that this heterogeneity is already present among individuals with prediabetes, with an opportunity for more targeted prevention before the onset of type 2 diabetes. [@wagner2026; @onthoni2025; @wagner2021].

Traditionally, risk stratification in prediabetes has relied on glycemic states, such as impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance, arising from a combination of insulin resistance and declining beta-cell function [@echouffo-tcheugui2021]. While glucose patterns during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) support our understanding of risk trajectories among individuals with prediabetes, they provide limited insights into more complex metabolic traits. Different measures of obesity, particularly visceral adipose tissue (VAT) and subcutaneous adipose tissue (SAT), are well-established risk factors for developing T2D. Consequently, body fat distribution represents a metabolic trait that is already present and differentiated among individuals in the prediabetic state [@philipsen2015; @borel2015]. This is underscored by recent, data-driven phenotyping, identifying subgroups with different characteristics, including diverse patterns of body fat distribution [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025].

Next up: Isolated effects og glp-1, gip and glucagon on obesity and prediabetes -\> something about how the interplay between glucose, insulin and hormones is not well understood -\> how this is assocaited to different measures of fat distribution

Notes from the protocol, maybe some of it will be used:

Beyond glucose responses, recent evidence indicates that dynamic hormonal responses to the OGTT, specifically those related to glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1), gastric inhibitory polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon may play an important role in progression to T2D. Moreover, it has recently been shown that body fat distribution varies across subtypes of individuals with prediabetes, with distinct patterns of visceral and hepatic fat influencing risk trajectories. However, the dynamic responses of glucose, insulin, incretins and glucagon during the OGTT, and how different patterns of fat distribution may be associated with these responses, remain unclear. Understanding these associations could improve identification of high-risk populations and facilitate targeted prevention strategies.
